text: Hello and welcome to This is My Architecture. Today I'm joined by Edwin from Cohesity.
Hi Marie. Hi Edwin. So let's start off. Can you tell me a little bit about Cohesity?
Sure. Cohesity provides a comprehensive data management platform for backup recovery,
DR, analytics, and security, which we call Cohesity Helios. And this platform is built on a modern
architecture software defined that can be deployed on premise in a customer's AWS account,
as well as consumers as a service, which we're talking about today, which we call data management as a service.
That's awesome. I'm really looking forward to hearing about data management as a service.
But you guys take a little bit of a different spin on it. Can you tell me more?
Definitely. Definitely. And what we did is with our D-managed services, we wanted to make sure
of an enterprise grade that's scalable, that will scale linearly and up, as well as, you know,
has flexibility for our customers to do, you know, data in whatever environment that they need to.
Yeah, that's great. And so let's dive in. Can you tell me a little bit about where we would start?
Yeah. So the control plane is the management plane, and it's built around microservices in EKS.
And the microservices allow our users to come in for the UI, you know, when they come in and use the service,
as well as it uses for internal processing, you know, when we're doing analytics for the data inside.
And, you know, it can scale from hundreds to thousands as we consume multiple petabytes of data
and process data, both internally, as well as externally.
Oh, that's great. Can you tell me a little bit more about those microservices?
Yeah, the microservices are leveraged for multiple ways. And we chose EKS because we want to add new services,
you know, when they come on board, but also the services are used to do data processing,
so customers can get billing information, analytics about their backups, as well as the UI
and how they manage their environment.
And so I see Elasticsearch and Aurora. So why are we using both?
Well, we're using both because we, customers' data comes in and we want to store it in Aurora.
And then what we want to do is continually analyze the data so that customers know about any type of alerts
or any type of analytics that you need to know on their backup.
And so how has Aurora and Elasticsearch made this architecture work for you guys and for your customers?
Yeah, I mean, having native services in AWS has helped us provide the information very quickly
and store it for a very amount of time, like 30 days, and continue to give customers updates
when they need it in real time.
So I see that we have PrivateLink and Transit Gateway next here on the architecture.
Tell me how that fits in.
Yeah, so we talked a little bit about the data plane. That's where we store it.
The customer stores our data. And any type of data we want to emphasize is secure.
So we're leveraging PrivateLinks to make sure that if it's an inter-region data plane,
that it's going over the PrivateLink and leveraging everything in a private VPC.
So we're leveraging VPC so it's staying internally and it's secure.
And then we have regions in other across the Americas, across different geographies,
and we use Transit Gateway in order to talk to those.
So everything uses the backbone for protection and speed.
And then what we're also doing is as the data is moving in between,
you're using the KMS to encrypt the data to make sure that, you know,
anything data in flight as well as stored is stored securely.
Yeah, that's great. So for PrivateLink and Transit Gateway,
say I want to have inter-region data as well as geo data. How does that work?
Yeah, it works really, really great. And the PrivateLink provides that secure connection between, you know,
if this is in the east and this data plane is in the east, it provides that PrivateLink.
So all the data is internally and it has the speed of the backbone.
And then if I'm going to a different region across the country or a different geo,
we're using Transit Gateway to make sure that data is secure and also leveraging the backbone of AWS.
Yeah, no, that's great. And so I want to take us to this final part of the data plane.
So I know you mentioned KMS. Obviously that's super important to make sure data is secure.
Can you talk to me a little bit about how we go between these two planes?
Yeah, definitely. So all of the control plane and data plane we've separated so we can scale,
have it modular configuration. And the data plane is leveraging EC2 and S3.
And what we do is when a customer subscribes to the service,
we're using a KMS to encrypt all the data that's moving across the link into the data plane,
as well as, you know, for each tenant, they have their own unique environment
that's provisioned automatically when they subscribe to the service.
So we can scale up literally and horizontally depending on the customer's use case.
Yeah, that's great. So kind of taking a look at this modern approach to data management as a,
or data management in general, what's most advantageous for your customers?
Really the most advantageous thing is to back up very quickly.
And we have customers who can do this in less than an hour.
So what typically took months or days or weeks, they can do very quickly and leverage the data,
have it very secure, scalable, and it can be globally procured.
Thanks for joining us today, Edwin.
I've learned a lot about data management as a service.
And thank you for watching. This is my architecture.
